edges.description: Loss of phosphotransferase activity impairs mannose-6-phosphate recognition-marker synthesis on lysosomal hydrolases.
GNPTAB alpha/beta subunit deficiency is measured as markedly decreased GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase activity.
Failed lysosomal delivery of degradative enzymes leaves macromolecules incompletely degraded inside cells.
The secretion/localization defect produces elevated plasma hydrolases and deficient intracellular enzyme activity.
Connective-tissue and skeletal storage contributes to the dominant skeletal and joint phenotype.
Cardiac and valvular tissues can accumulate storage material, contributing to valve and myocardial disease.
Residual ML III storage disease impairs childhood growth, producing postnatal growth slowing and short stature.
Storage in fibroblast-rich connective tissues contributes to facial, gingival, skin, abdominal wall, and ocular surface manifestations.
Cartilage, airway, middle-ear, and respiratory involvement connects lysosomal storage to recurrent otitis, hearing impairment, hoarse voice, and recurrent respiratory infections.
edges.target: Airway, middle ear, and hearing involvement

edges.description: Mild lysosomal storage in neurons and glia provides a mechanistic branch for developmental delay and cognitive impairment.
edges.target: Mild CNS storage and cognitive involvement

edges.description: Impaired lysosomal glycan degradation produces excessive urinary oligosaccharide excretion.
Glycosaminoglycan storage and turnover can present as urinary keratan sulfate excretion.
Joint stiffness is a very frequent skeletal/joint manifestation.
Bone pain is a frequent clinical consequence of skeletal disease.
Dysostosis multiplex is part of the radiographic skeletal phenotype.
Osteopenia and osteoporosis are part of the osteodystrophy.
Spinal skeletal involvement includes thoracolumbar kyphosis and scoliosis.
Progressive osteodystrophy includes severe carpal and femoral osteolysis.
Carpal tunnel syndrome is common in adults with ML III.
Progressive skeletal and joint disease can impair mobility and produce wheelchair dependence.
Joint disease causes hip, knee, and other flexion contractures that require physiotherapy.
Progressive skeletal and joint disease disturbs gait and mobility.
Skeletal and connective tissue involvement includes short neck morphology.
Mitral valve insufficiency is a frequent cardiac manifestation.
Aortic valve insufficiency is a frequent cardiac manifestation.
Progressive cardiorespiratory disease can culminate in heart failure.
Right ventricular structural involvement is an occasional cardiac feature.
Persistent postnatal growth slowing produces short stature.
Growth slowing after early childhood is captured clinically as postnatal growth retardation.
Connective-tissue storage can produce gingival enlargement.
Facial connective-tissue involvement produces gradual facial coarsening.
Craniofacial involvement includes epicanthal folds.
Craniofacial involvement includes full cheeks.
Skin fibroblast storage is consistent with thickened skin.
Connective-tissue and abdominal-wall involvement can produce umbilical hernia.
Abdominal-wall connective-tissue involvement can produce diastasis recti.
Abdominal-wall involvement can manifest as abdominal wall muscle weakness.
Craniofacial involvement includes depressed nasal bridge.
Craniofacial and orbital connective-tissue involvement can present as proptosis.
Ocular connective-tissue involvement can produce mild corneal clouding or opacity.
Middle-ear involvement causes recurrent otitis media.
edges.source: Airway, middle ear, and hearing involvement

edges.description: Recurrent middle-ear disease can cause conductive hearing impairment.
edges.source: Airway, middle ear, and hearing involvement

edges.description: Less commonly, hearing involvement includes sensorineural hearing impairment.

edges.description: Airway and laryngeal involvement can present with hoarse voice.
edges.source: Airway, middle ear, and hearing involvement

edges.description: Airway involvement predisposes to recurrent respiratory infections.

edges.description: Mild CNS storage and development effects can present as cognitive impairment.

edges.description: A larger extracellular-to-intracellular lysosomal hydrolase disparity reports more severe lysosomal enzyme mistargeting.
Lower measured GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase activity reports the primary GNPTAB alpha/beta enzyme defect.
Excess urinary oligosaccharide excretion is a nonspecific urine readout of impaired lysosomal glycan degradation.
Urinary keratan sulfate excretion reports excess glycosaminoglycan storage and turnover in this lysosomal storage disorder.

evidence.snippet: Mucolipidosis III alpha/beta (MLIII alpha/beta) is a lysosomal disorder characterized by progressive slowing of the growth rate from early childhood, stiffness and pain in joints, gradual coarsening of facial features, moderate developmental delay and mild intellectual disability in most patients.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotype and genotype in mucolipidoses II and III alpha/beta: a study of 61 probands
evidence.snippet: Fifty-one pathogenic changes in GNPTAB are presented, including 42 novel mutations.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic GNPTAB variants reduce activity of the alpha/beta subunits of UDP-N-acetylglucosamine-1-phosphotransferase, preventing normal synthesis of mannose-6-phosphate recognition markers on lysosomal hydrolases.

evidence.reference_title: Phenotype and genotype in mucolipidoses II and III alpha/beta: a study of 61 probands
evidence.snippet: The disorders are caused by mutations in GNPTAB, which encodes two of three subunits of the heterohexameric enzyme, N-acetylglucosamine-1-phosphotransferase.

evidence.reference_title: Mucolipidosis II and III alpha/beta: mutation analysis of 40 Japanese patients showed genotype-phenotype correlation.
evidence.snippet: diseases caused by a deficiency of alpha and/or beta subunits of the enzyme N-acetylglucosamine-1-phosphotransferase, which is encoded by the GNPTAB gene.

nodes.description: Incompletely degraded glycosaminoglycans, sphingolipids, and related macromolecules accumulate in lysosomes across connective tissue, cardiac, neural, cartilage, and fibroblast-rich tissues.

evidence.reference_title: The natural history and osteodystrophy of mucolipidosis types II and III.
evidence.snippet: global deficiencies of lysosomal degradative enzymes with concomitant intracellular accumulation of both partly degraded glycosaminoglycans and sphingolipids.

evidence.reference_title: Mucolipidosis type III alpha/beta: the first characterization of this rare disease by autopsy.
evidence.snippet: Accumulation of mucopolysaccharides along with lipids within lysosomes of patients with mucolipidosis is the characteristic abnormality.

nodes.description: Intravenous pamidronate has been reported as an adjunctive bisphosphonate treatment for ML III osteodystrophy and bone pain, improving pain and mobility in two siblings despite incomplete biochemical and histologic correction.

evidence.reference_title: The osteodystrophy of mucolipidosis type III and the effects of intravenous pamidronate treatment.
evidence.snippet: Intravenous pamidronate treatment given monthly for a year was well tolerated and produced dramatic clinical effects, with reduction in bone pain and improvements in mobility

evidence.reference_title: The osteodystrophy of mucolipidosis type III and the effects of intravenous pamidronate treatment.

nodes.description: ML III skeletal disease combines dysostosis multiplex, osteodystrophy, progressive osteoarthritis, cartilage destruction, bone lesions, bone pain, joint stiffness, and entrapment neuropathy.

evidence.reference_title: Mucolipidosis type III, a series of adult patients.
evidence.snippet: All patients had dysostosis multiplex and progressive secondary osteoarthritis, characterised by cartilage destruction and bone lesions in multiple joints.

evidence.reference_title: The osteodystrophy of mucolipidosis type III and the effects of intravenous pamidronate treatment.
evidence.snippet: Biochemical indices of bone turnover were increased, and transiliac bone biopsy demonstrated both trabecular osteopenia and marked subperiosteal bone resorption.

nodes.description: Childhood-onset ML III alpha/beta causes slowing of growth after early childhood and produces persistent short stature.

evidence.reference_title: Mucolipidosis III Alpha/Beta - RETIRED CHAPTER, FOR HISTORICAL REFERENCE ONLY.
evidence.snippet: a slowly progressive disorder with clinical onset at approximately age three years, is characterized by slow growth rate and subnormal stature

evidence.reference_title: Phenotype and genotype in mucolipidoses II and III alpha/beta: a study of 61 probands
evidence.snippet: ML III retains a low level of phosphotransferase activity because of at least one missense or splice site mutation. The phenotype is milder, with minimal delays in milestones, the appearance of facial coarsening by early school age, and slowing of growth after the age of 4 years.

nodes.description: Supportive management includes low-impact therapy, monitoring for skeletal, bone-pain, cardiac, respiratory, and airway/anesthesia complications, and follow-up frequency tailored to disease course.

evidence.reference_title: Mucolipidosis III Alpha/Beta - RETIRED CHAPTER, FOR HISTORICAL REFERENCE ONLY.
evidence.snippet: annual routine follow-up visits after age six years unless bone pain, deteriorating ambulation, and/or cardiac and respiratory monitoring need more frequent attention.
